lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I thought the king had more affected the Duke of Albany than Cornwall.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: It did always seem so to us: but now, in the division

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: of the kingdom, it appears not which of the dukes he values

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: most; for equalities are so weighed, that curiosity in neither can make

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: choice of either’s moiety.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Is not this your son, my lord?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: His breeding, sir, hath been at my charge: I have so often

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: blushed to acknowledge him, that now I am brazed to it.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I cannot conceive you.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Sir, this young fellow’s mother could: whereupon she grew round-wombed, and had,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: indeed, sir, a son for her cradle ere she had a husband

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: for her bed. Do you smell a fault?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I cannot wish the fault undone, the issue of it being so proper.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But I have, sir, a son by order of law, some year

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: elder than this, who yet is no dearer in my account: though

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: this knave came something saucily into the world before he was sent

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: for, yet was his mother fair; there was good sport at his

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: making, and the whoreson must be acknowledged. Do you know this noble

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: gentleman, Edmund?

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: No, my lord.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My lord of Kent: remember him hereafter as my honourable friend.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: My services to your lordship.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I must love you, and sue to know you better.

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: Sir, I shall study deserving.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He hath been out nine years, and away he shall again. The king is coming.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Attend the lords of France and Burgundy, Gloucester.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I shall, my liege.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Meantime we shall express our darker purpose.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Give me the map there. Know that we have divided

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: In three our kingdom: and ’tis our fast intent

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To shake all cares and business from our age;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Conferring them on younger strengths, while we

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Unburthen’d crawl toward death. Our son of Cornwall,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And you, our no less loving son of Albany,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: We have this hour a constant will to publish

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Our daughters’ several dowers, that future strife

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: May be prevented now. The princes, France and Burgundy,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Great rivals in our youngest daughter’s love,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Long in our court have made their amorous sojourn,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And here are to be answer’d. Tell me, my daughters—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Since now we will divest us both of rule,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Interest of territory, cares of state—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Which of you shall we say doth love us most?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That we our largest bounty may extend

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Where nature doth with merit challenge. Goneril,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Our eldest-born, speak first.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Sir, I love you more than words can wield the matter;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Dearer than eye-sight, space, and liberty;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Beyond what can be valued, rich or rare;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: No less than life, with grace, health, beauty, honour;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: As much as child e’er loved, or father found;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: A love that makes breath poor, and speech unable;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Beyond all manner of so much I love you.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: What shall Cordelia do? Love, and be silent.

lines.speaker: LEAR
lines.text: Of all these bounds, even from this line to this,

lines.speaker: LEAR
lines.text: With shadowy forests and with champains rich’d,

lines.speaker: LEAR
lines.text: With plenteous rivers and wide-skirted meads,

lines.speaker: LEAR
lines.text: We make thee lady: to thine and Albany’s issue

lines.speaker: LEAR
lines.text: Be this perpetual. What says our second daughter,

lines.speaker: LEAR
lines.text: Our dearest Regan, wife to Cornwall? Speak.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Sir, I am made

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Of the self-same metal that my sister is,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: And prize me at her worth. In my true heart

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: I find she names my very deed of love;

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Only she comes too short: that I profess

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Myself an enemy to all other joys,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Which the most precious square of sense possesses;

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: And find I am alone felicitate

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: In your dear highness’ love.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Then poor Cordelia!

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: And yet not so; since, I am sure, my love’s

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: More richer than my tongue.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To thee and thine hereditary ever

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Remain this ample third of our fair kingdom;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No less in space, validity, and pleasure,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Than that conferr’d on Goneril. Now, our joy,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Although the last, not least; to whose young love

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The vines of France and milk of Burgundy

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Strive to be interess’d; what can you say to draw

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: A third more opulent than your sisters? Speak.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Nothing, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Nothing!

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Nothing.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Nothing will come of nothing: speak again.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Unhappy that I am, I cannot heave

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: My heart into my mouth: I love your majesty

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: According to my bond; nor more nor less.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: How, how, Cordelia! mend your speech a little,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Lest it may mar your fortunes.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Good my lord,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: You have begot me, bred me, loved me: I

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Return those duties back as are right fit,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Obey you, love you, and most honour you.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Why have my sisters husbands, if they say

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: They love you all? Haply, when I shall wed,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: That lord whose hand must take my plight shall carry

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Half my love with him, half my care and duty:

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Sure, I shall never marry like my sisters,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: To love my father all.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: But goes thy heart with this?

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Ay, good my lord.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: So young, and so untender?

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: So young, my lord, and true.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Let it be so; thy truth, then, be thy dower:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: For, by the sacred radiance of the sun,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The mysteries of Hecate, and the night;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: By all the operation of the orbs

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: From whom we do exist, and cease to be;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Here I disclaim all my paternal care,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Propinquity and property of blood,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And as a stranger to my heart and me

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Hold thee, from this, for ever. The barbarous Scythian,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Or he that makes his generation messes

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To gorge his appetite, shall to my bosom

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Be as well neighbour’d, pitied, and relieved,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: As thou my sometime daughter.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Good my liege—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Peace, Kent!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Come not between the dragon and his wrath.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I loved her most, and thought to set my rest

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: On her kind nursery. Hence, and avoid my sight!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: So be my grave my peace, as here I give

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Her father’s heart from her! Call France; who stirs?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Call Burgundy. Cornwall and Albany,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: With my two daughters’ dowers digest this third:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Let pride, which she calls plainness, marry her.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I do invest you jointly with my power,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Pre-eminence, and all the large effects

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That troop with majesty. Ourself, by monthly course,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: With reservation of an hundred knights,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: By you to be sustain’d, shall our abode

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Make with you by due turns. Only we still retain

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The name, and all the additions to a king;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The sway, revenue, execution of the rest,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Beloved sons, be yours: which to confirm,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: This coronet part betwixt you.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Royal Lear,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Whom I have ever honour’d as my king,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Loved as my father, as my master follow’d,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: As my great patron thought on in my prayers—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The bow is bent and drawn, make from the shaft.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Let it fall rather, though the fork invade

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: The region of my heart: be Kent unmannerly,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: When Lear is mad. What wilt thou do, old man?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Think’st thou that duty shall have dread to speak,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: When power to flattery bows? To plainness honour’s bound,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: When majesty stoops to folly. Reverse thy doom;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: And, in thy best consideration, cheque

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: This hideous rashness: answer my life my judgment,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Thy youngest daughter does not love thee least;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Nor are those empty-hearted whose low sound

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Reverbs no hollowness.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Kent, on thy life, no more.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: My life I never held but as a pawn

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To wage against thy enemies; nor fear to lose it,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Thy safety being the motive.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Out of my sight!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: See better, Lear; and let me still remain

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: The true blank of thine eye.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Now, by Apollo—

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Now, by Apollo, king,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Thou swear’st thy gods in vain.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: O, vassal! miscreant! Laying his hand on his sword.

lines.speaker: ALBANY CORNWALL
lines.text: Dear sir, forbear.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Do:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Kill thy physician, and the fee bestow

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Upon thy foul disease. Revoke thy doom;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Or, whilst I can vent clamour from my throat,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I’ll tell thee thou dost evil.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Hear me, recreant!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: On thine allegiance, hear me!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Since thou hast sought to make us break our vow,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Which we durst never yet, and with strain’d pride

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To come between our sentence and our power,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Which nor our nature nor our place can bear,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Our potency made good, take thy reward.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Five days we do allot thee, for provision

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To shield thee from diseases of the world;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And on the sixth to turn thy hated back

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Upon our kingdom: if, on the tenth day following,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thy banish’d trunk be found in our dominions,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The moment is thy death. Away! by Jupiter,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: This shall not be revoked.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Fare thee well, king: sith thus thou wilt appear,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Freedom lives hence, and banishment is here.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To Cordelia. The gods to their dear shelter take thee, maid,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That justly think’st, and hast most rightly said!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To Regan and Goneril. And your large speeches may your deeds approve,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That good effects may spring from words of love.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Thus Kent, O princes, bids you all adieu;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: He’ll shape his old course in a country new.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Here’s France and Burgundy, my noble lord.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: My lord of Burgundy.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: We first address towards you, who with this king

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Hath rivall’d for our daughter: what, in the least,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Will you require in present dower with her,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Or cease your quest of love?

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Most royal majesty,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: I crave no more than what your highness offer’d,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Nor will you tender less.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Right noble Burgundy,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: When she was dear to us, we did hold her so;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: But now her price is fall’n. Sir, there she stands:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: If aught within that little seeming substance,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Or all of it, with our displeasure pieced,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And nothing more, may fitly like your grace,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: She’s there, and she is yours.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: I know no answer.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Will you, with those infirmities she owes,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Unfriended, new-adopted to our hate,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Dower’d with our curse, and stranger’d with our oath,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Take her, or leave her?

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Pardon me, royal sir;

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Election makes not up on such conditions.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Then leave her, sir; for, by the power that made me,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I tell you all her wealth. To King of France. For you, great king,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I would not from your love make such a stray,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To match you where I hate; therefore beseech you

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To avert your liking a more worthier way

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Than on a wretch whom nature is ashamed

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Almost to acknowledge hers.

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: This is most strange,

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: That she, that even but now was your best object,

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: The argument of your praise, balm of your age,

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Most best, most dearest, should in this trice of time

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Commit a thing so monstrous, to dismantle

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: So many folds of favour. Sure, her offence

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Must be of such unnatural degree,

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: That monsters it, or your fore-vouch’d affection

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Fall’n into taint: which to believe of her,

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Must be a faith that reason without miracle

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Could never plant in me.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: I yet beseech your majesty—

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: If for I want that glib and oily art,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: To speak and purpose not; since what I well intend,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: I’ll do’t before I speak—that you make known

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: It is no vicious blot, murder, or foulness,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: No unchaste action, or dishonour’d step,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: That hath deprived me of your grace and favour;

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: But even for want of that for which I am richer,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: A still-soliciting eye, and such a tongue

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: As I am glad I have not, though not to have it

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Hath lost me in your liking.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Better thou

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Hadst not been born than not to have pleased me better.

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Is it but this—a tardiness in nature

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Which often leaves the history unspoke

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: That it intends to do? My lord of Burgundy,

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: What say you to the lady? Love’s not love

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: When it is mingled with regards that stand

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Aloof from the entire point. Will you have her?

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: She is herself a dowry.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Royal Lear,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Give but that portion which yourself proposed,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: And here I take Cordelia by the hand,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Duchess of Burgundy.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Nothing: I have sworn; I am firm.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: I am sorry, then, you have so lost a father

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: That you must lose a husband.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Peace be with Burgundy!

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Since that respects of fortune are his love,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: I shall not be his wife.

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Fairest Cordelia, that art most rich, being poor;

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Most choice, forsaken; and most loved, despised!

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Thee and thy virtues here I seize upon:

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Be it lawful I take up what’s cast away.

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Gods, gods! ’tis strange that from their cold’st neglect

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: My love should kindle to inflamed respect.

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Thy dowerless daughter, king, thrown to my chance,

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Is queen of us, of ours, and our fair France:

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Not all the dukes of waterish Burgundy

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Can buy this unprized precious maid of me.

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Bid them farewell, Cordelia, though unkind:

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Thou losest here, a better where to find.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thou hast her, France: let her be thine; for we

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Have no such daughter, nor shall ever see

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That face of hers again. Therefore be gone

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Without our grace, our love, our benison.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Come, noble Burgundy.

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Bid farewell to your sisters.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: The jewels of our father, with wash’d eyes

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Cordelia leaves you: I know you what you are;

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: And like a sister am most loath to call

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Your faults as they are named. Use well our father:

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: To your professed bosoms I commit him

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: But yet, alas, stood I within his grace,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: I would prefer him to a better place.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: So, farewell to you both.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Prescribe not us our duties.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Let your study

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Be to content your lord, who hath received you

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: At fortune’s alms. You have obedience scanted,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: And well are worth the want that you have wanted.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Time shall unfold what plaited cunning hides:

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Who cover faults, at last shame them derides.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Well may you prosper!

lines.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
lines.text: Come, my fair Cordelia.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Sister, it is not a little I have to say of what

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: most nearly appertains to us both. I think our father will hence

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: to-night.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: That’s most certain, and with you; next month with us.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: You see how full of changes his age is; the observation we

stage_directions.text: Sennet. Enter King Lear, Cornwall, Albany, Goneril, Regan, Cordelia, and Attendants.
Flourish. Reenter Gloucester, with King of France, Burgundy, and Attendants.
Flourish. Exeunt all but King of France, Goneril, Regan, and Cordelia.
Exeunt King of France and Cordelia.

glosses.definition: the formal partition of the kingdom Lear plans to make
handsome result — Gloucester's wordplay on Edmund's conception
bastard — literally 'son of a whore,' used with affectionate crudeness here
a small crown — here the emblem of sovereignty Lear will physically divide
hidden design — Lear's secret plan for the division
separate dowries — the three shares of the kingdom
express it; put it into words
fertile flat plains; open countryside made wealthy
truthful affection — Regan echoes Goneril's formula
the favored child — Lear addresses Cordelia
Burgundian suitor (Duke of Burgundy) — dairy / wine as synecdoche for the two countries

glosses.phrase: Heave my heart into my mouth
glosses.definition: pour what I feel into speech — Cordelia's bitter refusal to flatter
dowry — Lear's gift to Cordelia, now withheld
the influence of the celestial spheres — astrology
barbarian — the Scythians were a byword for cruelty in classical antiquity
eats his own offspring — the extreme of cannibalistic cruelty
former daughter — Lear's disownment is explicit
loving care — Lear expected Cordelia to nurse him in age
the retinue of followers Lear keeps — a plot-point that will matter in 1.4 and 2.4
echoes no emptiness — Cordelia's love has substance, does not reverberate as boastful pretense does
Roman king of gods — Lear's oath by pagan divinity

glosses.phrase: dismantle / So many folds of favour
glosses.definition: strip away so many tokens of affection
France speaks — his love reveals itself against Lear's anger
France's paradoxes on Cordelia — valued the more for her lost dower
to you who claim to love him
Cordelia's wry name for her sisters

glosses.phrase: the best and soundest of his time

glosses.definition: deeply grafted temperament — his nature is fixed

glosses.phrase: shake all cares and business from our age
glosses.definition: shed all responsibilities of old age
by your oath of fealty — Lear's formal appeal

glosses.phrase: The best and soundest of his time hath been but rash
glosses.definition: even his best years were impulsive — Goneril's retrospective diagnosis
flaws of a character deeply grafted in

annotations.title: The play opens in prose — Gloucester and Kent on Edmund
annotations.body: Lear famously begins with a prose conversation between Kent and Gloucester about a bastard son, not with the titular king. Gloucester introduces Edmund as 'this knave came somewhat saucily to the world before he was sent for' — a casual genital joke delivered in his son's hearing. This opening does three structural jobs at once. It introduces the Gloucester subplot that will run in strict parallel to the main Lear plot. It establishes Edmund as the resentful outsider whose resentment the play will vindicate as he destroys everyone. And it makes sexual and familial irregularity the medium of the play's moral universe — the feudal order that is about to be formally divided was already rotten in private. Shakespeare's decision to open with Edmund's paternity rather than Lear's coronet is a quiet masterstroke.
sources: Jonathan Bate, Soul of the Age (2008)
Stephen Greenblatt, Will in the World (2004)

annotations.title: The love test — Lear's fatal category error
annotations.body: Lear's 'Which of you shall we say doth love us most' confuses two kinds of love — the emotional and the performative. Goneril and Regan understand the game; Cordelia refuses it. Her 'Nothing, my lord' answers a question that should not have been asked. The scene is often played as Lear's tragic blindness, but it is better understood as his tragic presumption: that love can be auctioned, that rank can be bargained for in affection. Lear wants both to give up the kingdom (physical power) and to keep all of love's ceremonies (emotional tribute). The play exists because those two things cannot coexist. Shakespeare, remarkably, gives Lear no good reason for staging the love test at all — no motive survives analysis except vanity itself. That is the point: vanity starts the tragedy.
sources: Stanley Cavell, 'The Avoidance of Love' in Disowning Knowledge (1987)

annotations.title: 'Nothing will come of nothing' — the play's governing word
annotations.body: Lear's 'Nothing will come of nothing: speak again' inaugurates a word that will return at the play's most critical junctures. Cordelia's 'Nothing' is Lear's undoing; Edmund's 'Nothing, my lord' in 1.2 (about the forged letter) is Gloucester's; the Fool in 1.4 calls the king 'nothing' — 'Now thou art an O without a figure.' In 4.6 the Fool is absent and Lear is himself reduced to the nothingness the word names. The line Lear speaks is an aggressive rhetorical pressure: 'say more, or I take back the gift.' Cordelia replies, essentially, by refusing the category: love cannot be spoken in the register Lear demands. The play's philosophical claim compresses here: the things that matter most cannot be turned into speech-acts without being destroyed in the process. This annotation sits here as the root, and every subsequent 'nothing' in the play is its fruit.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: Kent as the play's first truth-teller — and his immediate exile
annotations.body: When Lear exiles Cordelia, Kent steps in: 'Kill thy physician, and the fee bestow / Upon the foul disease.' He tells Lear his 'youngest daughter does not love thee least' — plain speech that costs him his rank and country. The play will make a pattern of this: those who speak truth are banished, blinded, or killed. The Fool, Kent, Cordelia, Gloucester, Edgar — each is punished for candor. Lear's world is built on the ceremonial lie, and the play will burn that world down around him. Kent's disguise as 'Caius' in 1.4 makes the point formal: the truth-teller can stay only if he performs as someone else. Shakespeare makes the political point sharp — a kingdom that cannot tolerate honesty is a kingdom that will not survive.

annotations.title: France sees Cordelia — love, unlike Lear's, that increases with loss
annotations.body: The Duke of Burgundy drops his suit the moment Cordelia loses her dowry. The King of France does the opposite: 'Fairest Cordelia, that art most rich, being poor; / Most choice, forsaken; and most lov'd, despis'd!' His love is the exact inverse of Lear's love-test economics. Where Lear measured love by gift received, France measures by gift given despite disfavor. The speech is the play's first real-time demonstration of what love actually is, offered against Lear's three-scene-long misunderstanding of it. It is also dramatically economical — we meet France only here, but this one speech is enough to make his absence in later acts painful when Cordelia comes back without him (4.3 sends him home on an 'imperfect' dispatch).
sources: Jill L. Levenson, 'Shakespeare's Language of Tragedy,' Cambridge Companion (2001)

trials.prompt: What is the significance of Cordelia's 'Nothing, my lord'?
options: She cannot speak from stage fright
She refuses to participate in Lear's flattery-for-land exchange — her answer rejects the terms of the question itself
She is angry with her sisters
She has forgotten her prepared speech

trials.prompt: The word 'nothing' is introduced in 1.1. How does it function across the play?
options: It appears only in this scene
It is an ordinary word that Shakespeare uses unremarkably
It returns as a governing refrain — Cordelia's 'Nothing,' Edmund's 'Nothing' (1.2), the Fool's 'thou art nothing' (1.4), and Lear's reduction to nothing in the storm scenes
It is always spoken by Lear

trials.prompt: Why does the play open with Gloucester's banter about Edmund rather than with Lear's coronet?
options: Shakespeare had not yet decided who the protagonist was
To introduce the Gloucester subplot as formal parallel to the main Lear plot, and to establish sexual/familial irregularity as the play's moral climate
It is an editorial error
To make the play shorter

trials.prompt: What does Kent try to do in response to Cordelia's banishment?
options: He agrees with Lear and withdraws
He intervenes on Cordelia's behalf — 'Thy youngest daughter does not love thee least' — and is himself exiled for speaking honestly
He convinces Lear to revoke the banishment
He leaves without a word

trials.prompt: What does France's willingness to marry dowry-less Cordelia demonstrate?
options: He is acting strategically for political alliance
Real love increases with loss, inverting Lear's transactional understanding of affection — the play's first positive demonstration of what love actually is
He is mocking her
He feels sorry for her

trials.prompt: The Fool, Kent, Cordelia, Gloucester, and Edgar share what common fate?
options: All of them are Lear's children
Each is punished for speaking truth — banished, blinded, disguised, or killed — in a play that systematically destroys its truth-tellers
None of them survive the play
All are nobility
